summary: Spolsky comments on Napster's failure to deliver a legal, paid music service despite having two years and a full development team, when the original Napster was built by one person in three months. He identifies this as a recurring pattern in software development where a single developer builds version 1 quickly, but a larger team fails to deliver version 2 for years.
key_points: Napster and Bertelsmann failed to deliver a legal paid music service despite two years of development
The original Napster was built by a single developer (Shawn Fanning) in just three months
This paradox — one person ships v1 fast, a team of 15 can't ship v2 — is a recurring pattern in software development
Adding people and corporate process to a project can slow it down rather than speed it up
money_quotes: One guy builds version 1 in a few months and then a team of 15 can't build version 2 for years and years. Tell me you haven't seen that movie before.
What I don't understand, exactly, is why they couldn't build this thing that sells Bertlesmann music with two whole years of work and a whole team of developers?
key_insight: A single motivated developer can often outship a large team, and scaling up headcount frequently produces the opposite of the intended effect — a pattern so common in software that it's practically a cliché.
